coredata.dc:title: Surface activity, micellization, and application of nano-surfactants—amphiphilic carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yin
Zhao, Mingwei
Lai, Lu
coredata.dc:description: For nano-surfactants, a new class of surfactants, the unique surface properties, micellization, and application of amphiphilic carbon dots (CDs) have been investigated carefully. Amphiphilic CDs (C12-CDs and C12-SCDs) were synthesized under mild reaction conditions. These CDs exhibit higher surface activity than conventional surfactants (APG12 and SDBS), whereas hydrophilic CDs have negligible surface activity. Above the critical micelle concentration, C12-CDs aggregate into an irregular spherical structure like micelle with an average diameter of about 100 nm, whereas C12-SCDs do not form aggregates. The surface tension of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs reaches the minimum at pH = 3, which is close to the isoelectric point of CDs. As pH increases, the water solubility of CDs increases, and thus the surface tension increases. C12-CDs can still maintain high activity at c NaCl = 250 g/L, but their resistance to CaCl2 is poor. The addition of the sulfonate groups enhances the tolerance of CDs to both NaCl and CaCl2. At c NaCl = 360 g/L and c CaCl2 = 750 g/L, C12-SCDs still have high surface activity. The removal efficiency of oil film and contact angle experiments show that compared to the traditional surfactant SDBS, C12-CDs and C12-SCDs have an excellent wettability alternation due to their abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and sulfonate groups. Among them, the wettability alternation of C12-SCDs is better than that of C12-CDs due to their hydrophilic sulfonate groups. Furthermore, the Pickering emulsions stabilized by C12-SCDs have rapid pH and CO2 response performance. In addition, compared to the traditional surfactants SDBS and APG12, CDs have an excellent dispersion performance to MWCNTs. Different from the dispersion mechanism of cationic surfactant CPC (strong electrostatic attraction), CDs are adsorbed on the surface of MWCNTs through their hydrophobic effect and π-π stacking effect to achieve effective dispersion. Among them, because the sulfonate groups have high steric resistance and electrostatic repulsion, the dispersion performance of C12-SCDs is better than that of C12-CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For nano-surfactants, a new class of surfactants, the unique surface properties, micellization, and application of amphiphilic carbon dots (CDs) have been investigated carefully. Amphiphilic CDs (C12-CDs and C12-SCDs) were synthesized under mild reaction conditions. These CDs exhibit higher surface activity than conventional surfactants (APG12 and SDBS), whereas hydrophilic CDs have negligible surface activity. Above the critical micelle concentration, C12-CDs aggregate into an irregular spherical structure like micelle with an average diameter of about 100 nm, whereas C12-SCDs do not form aggregates. The surface tension of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs reaches the minimum at pH = 3, which is close to the isoelectric point of CDs. As pH increases, the water solubility of CDs increases, and thus the surface tension increases. C12-CDs can still maintain high activity at c NaCl = 250 g/L, but their resistance to CaCl2 is poor. The addition of the sulfonate groups enhances the tolerance of CDs to both NaCl and CaCl2. At c NaCl = 360 g/L and c CaCl2 = 750 g/L, C12-SCDs still have high surface activity.

The removal efficiency of oil film and contact angle experiments show that compared to the traditional surfactant SDBS, C12-CDs and C12-SCDs have an excellent wettability alternation due to their abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and sulfonate groups. Among them, the wettability alternation of C12-SCDs is better than that of C12-CDs due to their hydrophilic sulfonate groups. Furthermore, the Pickering emulsions stabilized by C12-SCDs have rapid pH and CO2 response performance. In addition, compared to the traditional surfactants SDBS and APG12, CDs have an excellent dispersion performance to MWCNTs. Different from the dispersion mechanism of cationic surfactant CPC (strong electrostatic attraction), CDs are adsorbed on the surface of MWCNTs through their hydrophobic effect and π-π stacking effect to achieve effective dispersion. Among them, because the sulfonate groups have high steric resistance and electrostatic repulsion, the dispersion performance of C12-SCDs is better than that of C12-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Surfactants are amphiphilic molecules with the property of adsorbing onto the surfaces or interfaces and effectively reducing the surface or interfacial energies at a low concentration. These surfactants have a characteristic molecular structure simultaneously consisting of hydrophobic and hydrophilic headgroups. To obtain high surface activity or a unique function, researchers were exploring novel surfactants with different molecular structures, such as Gemini [1,2], Bola [3,4], and switchable surfactants [5,6]. In addition, the synergistic effects of various surfactants also attracted attention.

Presently, attributing to their small sizes and unique properties, the synthesis, properties, and application of nanomaterials have become a hot research topic with the development of nanotechnology [7–9]. As a kind of nanomaterial, amphiphilic nanoparticles have been investigated and reported. For example, hydrophilic SiO2 nanoparticles were hydrophobically modified using the silane coupling agent [10]. These modified amphiphilic nanoparticles were used to prepare a Pickering emulsion with more excellent stability than that of conventional emulsions. Another example is Janus particles, which possess two sides of different structures and/or chemical compositions. In addition, Janus particles exhibit unique amphiphilicity as compared to homogeneous particles due to their asymmetric design [11]. Although Janus particles have unusual surface/interfacial activity, the synthesis procedures of these nanoparticles are laborious and time-consuming and thus cannot be used widely nowadays. Therefore, exploring easily manufactured amphiphilic nanoparticles has an essential application value.

As a class of carbon nanomaterials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have garnered much interest as potential competitors to conventional semiconductor quantum dots [12–14]. CDs have the advantages of environmental friendliness, low cost, and simple synthetic routes and are thus superior to traditional semiconductor quantum dots. In addition to the optical properties, the amphiphilicity of CDs has attracted the attention of researchers. For example, Kaur et al. synthesized amphiphilic CDs using cationic surfactants—Cetylpyridinium bromide. These amphiphilic CDs were used for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+ ions [15]. Gu et al. reported a green and cost-effective hydrothermal route for synthesizing a series of CDs from readily available organics solvents, such as N, N′-dimethylformamide, and N, N′-dimethylacetamide, and these CDs were demonstrated to possess the amphiphilicity [16]. Pandit et al. synthesized the amphiphilic CDs using citric acid and phenylenediamine and used them as molecular probes [17]. Jiang used Tween 80 as the sole carbon source to synthesize white-emitting CDs with long alkyl chains. These long alkyl chains on the CDs surface suppressed the aggregation-caused quenching effect and enhanced interaction with lipophilic substances. Thus, these CDs could be used as label-free imaging of latent fingerprints [18]. Chen et al. prepared amphiphilic CDs from natural Chinese paprika and achieved the rapid crossing of the cell membrane by regulating the hydrophilic-lipophilic balance of CDs [19]. However, these reports focused on and took advantage of the amphiphilicity of CDs to detect, analyze, and bioimage rather than the surface/interfacial properties and micellization behavior of amphiphilic CDs.

In 2014, Yang et al. proposed the use of graphene quantum dots as efficient surfactants to stabilize the Pickering emulsion [20]. They first observed the surfactant-like behavior of GQDs with tailored surface properties. Subsequently, in 2019, Cui et al. used citric acid and 3-(dimethylamino)-1-propylamine to synthesize GQDs and then reacted with chlorohexadecane to obtain the amphiphilic GQDs, which have been considered cationic surfactants. The results demonstrated that the surface tension could be reduced to 32 mN/m at 0.7 mg/mL [21]. In the same year, Xi et al. synthesized the amphiphilic graphene quantum dots using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and sodium laurate as raw materials at 200 °C for 10 h [22]. They found that these amphiphilic GQDs exhibit similar properties as surfactants, such as stabilizing Pickering emulsion, self-assemble into the self-supporting monolith, dispersing hydrophobic carbon nanotubes to form ultra-light aerogel, and promoting the fast-spreading of water on a superhydrophobic surface. A recent report indicated that the performances of amphiphilic CDs are superior to several conventional cationic surfactants [23]. Although some reports have already paid attention to the surface/interfacial activity of amphiphilic CDs, the corresponding studies are still inadequate. As a new class of surfactants named nano-surfactants, the unique surface/interfacial properties of amphiphilic CDs should be investigated carefully. Therefore, we synthesized a series of amphiphilic CDs using a straightforward one-pot method and common raw materials, which have also been used to synthesize conventional surfactants. The surface properties and micellization behavior were then investigated carefully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfamic acid (99.5%), hexadecylpyridinium chloride monohydrate (CPC, 99%), Sudan Ⅳ (Biological stain), and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs, 95%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS, 99%) was purchased from Qingdao Yousuo Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Lauryl glucoside (APG12, 50%) was purchased from Yuan Ye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0%) and other organic solvents (AR) were provided by Chengdu Kolon Chemical Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amphiphilic and hydrophilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The amphiphilic CDs were synthesized through a simple synthetic route, as shown in Fig. 1 . Taking C12-CDs as an example, citric acid (2.0 g) and dodecylamine (2.0 g) were added to 50 mL of three-neck flasks and heated at 180 °C for 3 h in the oil bath. The obtained products were then dissolved in absolute ethanol (50 mL). Next, the CDs solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the undissolved substances. After removing ethanol by reduced pressure distillation, the products were dissolved in ultrapure water and dialyzed with a 1 kDa dialysis membrane for 12 h. Finally, the C12-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying. The dodecylamine was replaced with hexylamine, n-octylamine, and ethylenediamine, respectively, and C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and hydrophilic CDs (H-CDs) were prepared using the same method.

To enrich the surface functional groups of amphiphilic CDs, we further sulfonated C12-CDs. C12-CDs (0.5 g) and sulfamic acid (0.5 g) were added to methanol (50 mL) and refluxed at 70 °C for 5 h. After removing methanol by reduced pressure distillation, the dried crude product was dissolved in ethanol, filtered, and dried to obtain sulfonated amphiphilic CDs (C12-SCDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The infrared spectra of amphiphilic CDs were obtained using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Nicolet, USA). The sample was prepared by the KBr disc technique, with a scanning range of 500–4000 cm−1. Next, the automatic elemental analyzer (Flyer UNICUBE, Elementar, Germany) was used to examine different element contents. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with the X-ray source of Al Kα (hv = 1486.6 eV) was employed to explore the elemental composition and corresponding chemical valence states of amphiphilic CDs. The survey and core-level spectra were recorded under the pass energy of 20 and 100 eV, respectively. The standard C 1 s with a binding energy of 284.8 eV was selected as a reference. Field-emission transmission electron microscopy analysis (TEM) was performed using JEM-2100 F (JEOL, Japan), operating at 200 kV with the superthin carbon coat. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were obtained with the tapping mode by a Bruker Dimension Icon (Bruker. Inc, USA). First, a small amount of CDs was dispersed in ethanol for ultrasonic treatment to obtain a low concentration of CDs solution. The CDs solution was then dropped onto the clean silicon wafer, and the AFM test was carried out after drying. The scanning frequency was 1 Hz. Thermogravimetric analysis was conducted on a TGA reactor (TGA 550, TA, USA) to investigate the thermal stability of the synthesized amphiphilic CDs. In each test, 10 mg of sample was loaded in an alumina crucible and heated from 30 °C to 1000 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C/min at the N2 atmosphere. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of CDs with different excitation wavelengths in the range of 370–550 nm were investigated by a fluorescence spectrometer (LS55, PerkinElmer, USA). The excitation slit and emission slit were 5.0 nm, and the scanning speed was 500 nm/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of surface tension curves, dynamic interfacial tension curves, and micellization behavior of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Equilibrium surface tension of CDs solution was determined on a surface tensiometer (K11, Krüss, Germany) through the Wilhelmy plate method. The interfacial tension between CDs aqueous solution and crude oil was measured by the spinning drop interfacial tensiometer (TX500C, POWEREACH, China). The hydrodynamic diameters and zeta potentials of CDs solutions were determined using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Panalytical, UK).

Sudan IV is a common lipophilic dye used for solubilization experiments. Excess Sudan IV was added to CDs or surfactant aqueous solutions and then magnetically stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The solution was centrifuged at 3,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the undissolved Sudan IV. The absorbance of the solution was measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TU1810, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument, China) at the maximum absorption wavelength (λ = 517.0 nm) of Sudan IV.

The morphology images of aggregates under dry conditions were observed by TEM. Among them, the negative staining method was used to prepare samples when observing the enlarged morphology of C12-CDs aggregates. First, the solution was dropped onto the superthin carbon coat. Then, after drying, the sample was dyed with phosphotungstic acid. The samples were tested by TEM after the dye was dried with a filter paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of oil film removal efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The surface of the glass sheets after lipophilic treatment was coated with crude oil and aged at 100 °C for 48 h to simulate the oil film on the rock surface of the reservoir. The glass sheets were immersed in SDBS and CDs solutions (500 mg/L), respectively, and the oil film removal was recorded at different treatment times. The relative area of residual oil film at different times was obtained by the graphical analysis software. The contact angles of glass sheets before and after the treatment of SDBS and CDs solution were measured by the optical contact angle measuring instrument (DSA100, Krüss, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pickering emulsion stabilized by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The volume ratio of toluene to CDs aqueous solutions (2.0 mg/mL) was 2:3. The oil/water mixtures were then homogenized with a high-speed rotor (F25, FlUKO, German) at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The pH-response reversibility of Pickering emulsion was studied by adding HCl or NaOH aqueous solution (0.1 mol/L) to adjust the pH values. Moreover, the emulsion droplets were observed by optical microscopy (BM1800, NOVEL, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of dispersion properties of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Two amphiphilic CDs, cationic surfactant CPC, anionic surfactant SDBS, and nonionic surfactant APG12, were used to disperse the MWCNTs, respectively. The concentration of CDs or surfactants was 500 mg/L, while the concentration of MWCNTs was 0.1%. After ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, MWCNTs suspension was obtained and allowed to stand for 24 h for subsequent characterization. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of each sample were measured by taking a certain amount of the upper suspension and diluting it 100 times. The corresponding CDs or surfactant solution was used as a reference solution for each measurement.

The suspension was centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 10 min, and the zeta potential of MWCNTs before and after CDs or surfactant treatment at pH = 7.0 was measured using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Panalytical, UK). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, MIRA3, Tescan, Czech Republic) was used to observe the microscopic morphology of MWCNTs. The upper suspension was dropped onto the aluminum foil, dried, and placed on the carbon conductive tape to conduct the measurements. SEM measurement was carried out using a 10 kV electron beam. MWCNTs aerogel was prepared by freeze-drying the MWCNTs suspension dispersed by CDs. The specific surface area of MWCNTs aerogel was analyzed by an automatic specific surface and porosity analyzer (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics, USA) at 393.15 K.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs was observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 (a1) and (b1), both C12-CDs and C12-SCDs have good dispersion with a size distribution between 1.0 nm and 4.0 nm, and the average sizes of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs are 2.6 nm and 2.0 nm, respectively. Furthermore, there is a slight change in the particle size and morphology of CDs after sulfonation, which shows that sulfonation does not damage the structure of C12-CDs. In Fig. 2(a2) and (b2), a lattice diffraction fringe of 0.19 nm could be observed close to the (1120) crystal plane of graphene. AFM shows that the typical thickness of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs is ∼1.80 nm and ∼1.73 nm, respectively (Fig. 2(a3) and (b3)), indicating that the two amphiphilic CDs are a few layers.

Table 1 shows different element contents of CDs. The presence of element S confirms the success of the sulfonation reaction. Correspondingly, elements N and O increase, and element C decreases. To better analyze the changes of functional groups on the surface of CDs before and after the sulfonation reaction, the infrared and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) characterized the elemental and functional group compositions of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), element C of C12-CDs is composed of four structures: π-π* (289.34 eV), CO (288.38 eV), C–C/CC (284.80 eV), and C–N (286.06 eV). The existence of π-π* proves the successful synthesis of CDs. The presence of CC confirms the existence of graphite structure in C12-CDs. FTIR spectra in Fig. S1(a) shows that the two firm peaks at 2926 cm−1 and 2856 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of the C–H bond, and the difference in these peak intensities of the three CDs can be seen. The amide bonding from the stretching vibration of the CO bond and the bending peak of the N–H bond appear separately at 1696 cm−1 and 1645 cm−1. The characteristic stretch of the amide Ⅲ C–N bond is at 1542 cm−1, indicating that these amide functional groups successfully introduce the long alkyl amine chain in amphiphilic CDs.

Fig. S2 shows the thermogravimetric analysis results of C12-CDs. It can be seen that a large amount of mass loss occurs between 220.21 °C and 331.45 °C. The mass change in this temperature range is due to the dissociation of oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl, which is consistent with the result that C12-CDs contain a large number of oxygen-containing groups [25]. Subsequently, a mass loss occurs in the range of 331.45 °C–529.04 °C, which the dissociation of dodecylamine might cause [26]. When the temperature reaches 492.37 °C, the residual mass decreases slowly with the increase in temperature, attributed to the graphene-like structure in C12-CDs [27]. Therefore, the synthesized C12-CDs have high-temperature resistance characteristics and can be applied below 220 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface activity and micellization of amphiphilic CDs
Surface tension curves of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a) shows that the amphiphilic CDs exhibit similar surface tension curves to the conventional surfactants. Specifically, as the concentration of CDs increases, the surface tension decreases and attains a particular value. According to the breakpoint of the surface tension curves, the critical micelle concentrations (CMC) of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs are 5,000 mg/L, 2,500 mg/L, and 100 mg/L, respectively. At CMC, the surface tension (γ CMC) of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs are 37.82 mN/m, 34.62 mN/m, and 27.45 mN/m, respectively. The increase in the alkyl chain length reduces the CMC and γ CMC, resulting from the increase in the hydrophobicity of CDs.

Fig. 4(b) shows that the hydrophilic CDs (H-CDs) do not reduce surface tension. Furthermore, the conventional surfactants, APG12 and SDBS, were selected for comparison. C12-CDs have a low CMC and γCMC, thus indicating a higher surface activity than APG12 and SDBS. Fig. 4(c) shows that the CMC of C12-SCDs is 200 mg/L and γCMC is 28.41 mN/m. Therefore, C12-SCDs and C12-CDs are nanoparticles with high surface activity, resulting from the amphiphilicity of CDs. Table 2 shows the surface tension of amphiphilic CDs synthesized in this paper and reported by some references [21–23,28] and indicates that C12-SCDs and C12-CDs have the lowest CMC and γ CMC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micellization of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surfactant molecules will aggregate as the concentration increases to form micelles of different shapes, such as spheric micelles [29], rod-shaped micelles [30], and vesicles [31]. To understand the micellization behavior of amphiphilic CDs, we determined the sizes of CDs aggregates under different conditions using dynamic light scattering (DLS). DLS size distribution can be depicted by number, intensity, or volume. According to the Rayleigh equation, the intensity of scattered light is directly proportional to the sixth power of the particle size. Therefore, we mainly analyzed the size distribution by the number under different conditions, while the size distribution by intensity was used as a supplement.

In Fig. 5 (a), the number peak shows that the average size of C12-CDs is about 5 nm at 0.1CMC, which is slightly larger than that of TEM images. Given that the DLS result is the hydrodynamic size of particles, it is not difficult to understand that the DLS size is somewhat larger than the particle size obtained from TEM images. Therefore, C12-CDs exist mainly as single particles at 0.1CMC. However, from the intensity peak, we find a few aggregates with an average size of ∼200 nm at 0.1CMC, which cannot be observed at the number peak. Therefore, the number of large-sized aggregates is minimal and could be neglected. When the C12-CDs concentration increases to 0.5CMC, the average size increases to ∼30 nm, indicating that C12-CDs aggregate into tiny micelles, six times as big as single C12-CDs particles. Above 0.5CMC, the number and intensity plots are unimodal, and the average size of C12-CDs aggregates at CMC increases to ∼60 nm. As the C12-CDs concentration increases from CMC to 50CMC, the aggregate size increases from ∼60 nm to ∼90 nm.

Fig. 5(b) shows the DLS size distribution of C12-SCDs at different concentrations. The average size of C12-SCDs at 0.1CMC is similar to that of C12-CDs, about 3 nm. However, the average size of C12-SCDs at 0.5CMC is much larger than other concentrations. As the concentration increases to 10CMC, it gradually decreases to 6 nm, close to the size of a single particle in TEM images and far smaller than the size of C12-CDs aggregates. However, from the intensity peak, we find some large-sized particles with an average size of about 105 nm. Interestingly, this size is similar to that of C12-CDs aggregates. Therefore, we speculate that C12-SCDs do not form aggregates, and the large-sized particles in their intensity peaks might be unsulfonated C12-CDs aggregates.

To verify the above conjecture, we chose the lipophilic dye Sudan IV for solubilization experiments. After the surfactant forms micelles in an aqueous solution, it can encapsulate the water-insoluble dye into the micelles so that the solubility of the dye increases significantly [32]. Therefore, the solubilization experiment is important to judge the existence of micelles. Fig. 5(c) shows the relationship between the CDs or surfactant concentration and absorbance (λ = 517.0 nm) of Sudan IV solubilized by two conventional surfactants (sodium laurate, SDBS), C12-CDs, and C12-SCDs. It can be seen from Fig. 5(c) that the order of solubilizing ability to Sudan IV is C12-CDs > APG > SDBS > C12-SCDs. The solubilization sites are generally located in micelles for macromolecular organic dyes such as Sudan IV, which are not easy to polarize [33]. Therefore, the number and size of micelles will directly affect Sudan IV solubilization. That is, the smaller the CMC, the more the number of micelles, and the greater the solubilization of Sudan IV. Therefore, the results of CMC measurement (CMCC12-CDs > CMCAPG > CMCSDBS) are consistent with the results of Sudan IV solubilization experiment. However, C12-SCDs have little solubilization ability, which verifies our conjecture that C12-SCDs do not form micelles.

Fig. 6 shows transmission electron micrographs of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs at 10CMC. The C12-CDs aggregates in Fig. 6(a) are uniformly distributed and have a size of about 70 nm. However, no large-sized aggregates are observed in Fig. 6(b), further verifying our conjecture. Neto et al. investigated the aggregation behavior of graphene oxide (GO) in water using the Potential of Mean Force (PMF) calculations, indicating that the increase in the oxygen content reduced the tendency for spontaneous aggregation of GO [34]. In addition, Paloncyova et al. constructed CDs models by MD simulations to analyze the effects of functional groups on the behavior of CDs in water [35]. Results suggested that carboxyl groups interconnect the adjacent layers and decrease the rate of internal rotations. According to the elemental analysis results in Table 1, oxygen content in C12-SCDs is twice as high as that in C12-CDs. Therefore, we speculate that the high oxygen content of the sulfonated C12-SCDs and excellent spatial resistance of sulfonate groups are unfavorable for the aggregate forming of C12-SCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on surface activity of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 7(a) and 8(a)
show the change in surface tension of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs solution with pH at 25 °C, respectively. At 0.1CMC, the pH value significantly affects the surface tension of these two CDs, and the effect degree gradually decreases as the concentration increases. At pH = 3, the surface tension reaches the minimum value. The surface tension of these two CDs increases obviously with the increased pH values. Since CDs surfaces are rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, and sulfonate groups, the deprotonation of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and sulfonate groups under an alkaline environment increases the hydrophilicity of CDs and decreases the interfacial CDs concentration, resulting in high surface tension [36]. However, with a further pH decrease from 3, the proportion of protonated carboxyl groups increases, the solubility of C12-CDs solution decreases, and even some C12-CDs begin to precipitate (Fig. S4), resulting in high surface tension. Nevertheless, as shown in Fig. 8 (a), the carboxyl content of sulfonated C12-SCDs is lower than that of C12-CDs, so C12-SCDs can still maintain high surface activity in a strongly acidic environment.

As shown in Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 8(b), the isoelectric points of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs are 3.9 and 3.1, respectively. The zeta potential of both CDs decreases with the increase in pH, resulting from the ionization degree change of the hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and sulfonate groups on the CDs surface. At the isoelectric point, the electrostatic repulsion force between CDs is close to zero, and CDs are easy to agglomerate. Below the isoelectric point, the groups are protonated and show positive electricity, whereas CDs show a negative charge above the isoelectric point [37].

Fig. 7(c) shows that when pH is below 5, the average particle size of C12-CDs aggregates becomes smaller at 10CMC, and an intensity peak with a large particle size appears. It is because, at low pH, the carboxyl groups on the surface of C12-CDs are protonated, which directly leads to the loss of water solubility of some C12-CDs; hence, the intensity peak with large particle size appears. On the other hand, when pH is above 5, the absolute value of the zeta potential of CDs is large, and the electrostatic repulsion force between the CDs is sufficient to maintain their stable dispersion in water. Therefore, when pH is between 5 and 11, the particle size of C12-CDs aggregates is changed slightly, and both the number peak and the intensity peak are unimodal.

In Fig. 8(c), at pH = 1, C12-SCDs do not have a similar situation in that the particle size of C12-CDs aggregates becomes small. Because the carboxyl content of sulfonated C12-SCDs is lower than that of C12-CDs, its water solubility is less affected by pH value. The appearance of C12-SCDs aggregates with a larger size is also due to the decrease in the zeta potential. Fig. S3 shows that pH has little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the two CDs, indicating that strong acid and base will not damage the luminescent structure of the CDs core.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature on surface activity of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 (a) and (b) show the effects of temperature on the surface tension of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs at different concentrations. The surface tension of both C12-CDs and C12-SCDs decreases as the temperature increases from 298.15 K to 343.15 K. Increasing the temperature decreases the hydrogen bond interaction between the hydroxyl groups of CDs and water, which increases the hydrophobicity of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs [38]. Compared to the C12-CDs, the surface tension of C12-SCDs exhibits a slight change with the increase in temperature. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 9(c) and (d), the temperature does not affect the size of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs aggregates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of inorganic salts on surface activity of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The salt-resistance property is critical for the application of surfactants. In Fig. 10 , we investigate the effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the surface tension of C12-CDs and C12-SCDs. Fig. 10(a) shows that the surface tension of C12-CDs decreases with the NaCl concentration and attains a certain value at c NaCl above 100 g/L, implying a high Na+ resistance property. However, the addition of CaCl2 sharply increases the surface tension of C12-CDs at c CaCl2 = 0.8 g/L (Fig. 10(b)). As shown in Fig. 10(c) and (d), the introduction of sulfonate groups improves the salt resistance property of CDs to a great extent. At c NaCl < 360 g/L and c CaCl2 < 500 g/L, C12-SCDs exhibits higher surface activity than that without inorganic salt. Until the c CaCl2 increases from 500 g/L to 750 g/L, the surface tension of C12-SCDs increases. As a result, C12-SCDs can be used in a harsh environment to achieve excellent surface activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of amphiphilic CDs as surfactants
Oil film removal of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The rock surface of the hydrophilic sandstone reservoir will be altered to lipophilic after soaking in crude oil for a long time. After conventional water flooding, there is still residual oil film on the surface of the lipophilic rock [39]. Therefore, it is essential to improve oil recovery by controlling the wettability of rock. The surfactant interacts with the surface of the lipophilic rock through its hydrophobic tail, and the hydrophilic headgroups cover the surface of the lipophilic rock, making the surface turn to water-wet [40]. At the same time, it increases the electrostatic repulsion between the rock surface and the oil film and improves the oil removal efficiency [41]. The amphiphilic CDs have amine, hydroxyl, carboxyl, sulfonate, and long hydrophobic chains simultaneously, which can realize the transition from oil-wet to water-wet and promote the removal efficiency of the oil film.

As shown in Fig. 11 (a), after treatment with CDs solution, the area of the oil film decreases significantly, and the oil film shrinks and separates from the rock surface in the form of oil beads. Furthermore, the removal efficiency of C12-SCDs is better than that of C12-CDs. However, after treatment with SDBS solution, the oil film area is only slightly reduced, and most of the oil film is still not effectively removed after 24 h. The relative area change of oil film with time is shown in Fig. 11(b). After treatment with C12-SCDs and C12-CDs solutions, the oil film area decreases by 89% and 56% within 24 h, respectively. In contrast, the area decrease of the oil film in SDBS solution is merely about 33%. Fig. 11(c) shows that the interfacial tension between C12-SCDs aqueous solution and crude oil is slightly lower than that of C12-CDs, but the difference is insignificant. It shows that the interfacial tension is not the main reason that affects the removal efficiency of the oil film. The contact angles of glass sheets before and after treatment with CDs and SDBS solution are shown in Fig. 11(d). C12-SCDs decrease the contact angle from 106.3° to 26.2°, while the contact angle decreases from 102.1° to 37.3° after C12-CDs treatment. It shows that the wettability regulation of the oil-solid interface plays a vital role in oil film removal.

The mechanism of oil film removal by CDs is analyzed as follows: firstly, CDs adsorb on the oil-water interface and reduce the oil-water interfacial tension; Secondly, after the adsorption of CDs on the rock surface, the amine, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and sulfonate groups of CDs change the rock surface from oil-wet to water-wet. Because CDs adsorb at the oil-water interface and the rock surface simultaneously, the electrostatic repulsion force between the stripped oil droplets and the rock surface increases, making it difficult for the stripped oil film to adhere to the glass surface again and realize efficient removal. In addition, because C12-SCDs alter the rock surface from oil-wet to strong water-wet, which significantly increases the contact angle of oil droplets on the rock surface and reduces the adhesion work, making the C12-SCDs have better removal performance of oil film than C12-CDs [42–46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversible emulsion induced by pH and CO2
ce:section.ce:para: According to the effects of pH on the surface activity of C12-SCDs, we investigated the pH-response reversibility of the Pickering emulsion stabilized by C12-SCDs. As shown in Fig. 12 , the emulsion disappears rapidly without vibration when the pH changes from neutral to acidic with the addition of HCl. The protonation of C12-SCDs increases their hydrophobicity; thus, the fluorescence can be observed in the toluene phase. If the pH value is adjusted to neutral by adding NaOH aqueous solution, the two phases could form stable emulsion again after shaking. The emulsion disappears rapidly when the pH value is adjusted from neutral to alkaline with the further addition of NaOH solution. At this time, C12-SCDs are deprotonated, and the fluorescence mainly exists in the aqueous phase. The self-assembly of C12-SCDs under low and high pH conditions can be easily reversed by adjusting the pH value of the solution back to neutral. The mechanism is shown in Fig. 13 . The pH-response demulsification-emulsification process is reversible, and the number of repetitions of the reversible cycle depends on the formation of ionic strength in the aqueous solution during the acid-base cycle (NaOH + HCl = NaCl + H2O) [47]. Due to the shielding of electrostatic repulsive interaction between nanoparticles [48], the increased ionic strength leads to the formation of an unstable dispersion of emulsion (as shown in Fig. S5). After these two cycles, the emulsification behavior does not change, indicating excellent recoverability. Therefore, C12-SCDs used as a Pickering emulsifier can be used for sustainable operation due to the recyclability of the emulsifier. These results suggest that the pH-responsive Pickering emulsion stabilized by C12-SCDs has great potential in many practical sustainable applications, such as enhanced oil recovery and oil/water separation.

Similar to the above mechanism, the stability of the Pickering emulsion can also be controlled by alternately using CO2 or N2 bubbling. When bubbling with CO2, the pH of the solution decreases, and the stable Pickering emulsion appears the phase separation. The micrograph (Fig. S6) shows that large-sized oil droplets appear, and the emulsion becomes uneven. When bubbling or heating with N2, the layered solution becomes a uniform emulsion again. However, because the pH of the saturated CO2 solution is about 3–4, the degree of carboxyl protonation is not enough, and complete demulsification does not occur. In conclusion, the Pickering emulsion stabilized by C12-SCDs is also CO2-responsive.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion of MWCNTs using amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: MWCNTs are favored in chemistry, biology, medicine, materials, and energy due to their unique structure, sound surface effects, and excellent thermal, electromagnetic, and mechanical properties. The carbon six-membered ring structure of MWCNTs makes many highly delocalized π electrons generated between surface carbon atoms through sp2 hybrid orbitals, which makes adjacent MWCNTs attract each other under the action of π–π stacking. Moreover, MWCNTs have a large specific surface area, and the particles easily agglomerate due to the strong van der Waals force, which reduces the surface energy [49,50]. Therefore, the effective dispersion of MWCNTs has become a critical problem to be solved. As shown in Fig. 14 (a), the amphiphilic CDs can be utilized to achieve stable dispersion of MWCNTs without changing their structure.

The UV–Vis spectra are an important method to characterize the dispersion of MWCNTs [51,52]. The higher the absorbance, the better is the dispersion of MWCNTs. Fig. 14(b) shows the absorbance of MWCNTs suspension dispersed by different CDs and surfactants at λ = 260.0 nm in the order of CPC > C12-SCDs > C12-CDs > SDBS > APG.

The zeta potentials of CPC, SDBS, C12-CDs, and C12-SCDs aqueous solutions are 14.31 mV, −14.58 mV, −9.89 mV, and −13.56 mV, respectively. APG is a nonionic surfactant; hence it is not measured. No zeta potential is detected in the supernatant of the corresponding surfactant–MWCNTs sample after centrifugation, indicating no obvious micelles are produced. This also shows that the adsorption on the surface of MWCNTs significantly reduces the excess surfactant in the liquid phase. At the same time, the possible influence of surfactant-charged micelles on the zeta potential determination of the MWCNTs system can be excluded.

The zeta potential of MWCNTs is measured to be −9.37 mV, while the zeta potentials of MWCNTs after surfactant modification are shown in Fig. 14(c). Among them, the zeta potentials of MWCNTs modified by C12-CDs, C12-SCDs, CPC, SDBS, and APG are −22.5 mV, −35.9 mV, 29.20 mV, −21.86 mV, and −11.72 mV, respectively. The absolute value of the zeta potential of the C12-SCDs–MWCNTs is the largest. According to the DLVO theory, the larger the absolute value of the surface potential of the suspended particles in the dispersion system, the greater is the electrostatic repulsion energy between the particles and the better is the thermodynamic stability of the suspension.

The SEM images of MWCNTs before and after dispersion by amphiphilic CDs are shown in Fig. S7. The original MWCNTs without CDs are agglomerated, and the entanglement between tubes is severe (Fig. S7(a)). As shown in Figs. S7(b) and (c), the dispersion performance of MWCNTs dispersed by amphiphilic CDs has been improved. More MWCNTs have been stripped from the cluster, and the aggregates have been significantly reduced. The SEM results are consistent with the previous UV–Vis absorption spectra and zeta potential measurements. It indicates that these two amphiphilic CDs could achieve good dispersion of MWCNTs, and the dispersion effect of C12-SCDs is better than that of C12-CDs.

Compared to anionic surfactant SDBS and nonionic surfactant APG, the positive headgroups of cationic surfactant CPC are adsorbed to the negative surface of MWCNTs through stronger electrostatic attraction. Hence, its absolute value of surface zeta-potential is greater than that of the other two traditional surfactants. Because of their excellent conjugated structure, CDs can generate strong attraction through π-π stacking with MWCNTs. Meanwhile, the rich hydrophobic alkyl chains of CDs are adsorbed to the surface of MWCNTs through van der Waals force and hydrophobic force, making them more firmly wrapped on MWCNTs, effectively preventing the agglomeration between MWCNTs, and ensuring the stability of the dispersion system. The sulfonated amphiphilic CDs (C12-SCDs) contain more abundant anionic headgroups, resulting in excellent steric hindrance and more muscular electrostatic repulsion energy [51–53]. Therefore, it has a better dispersion performance than C12-CDs.

To further prove that amphiphilic CDs can be used as dispersants to stabilize MWCNTs, the suspension is freeze-dried to prepare aerogel. These two aerogels have a low density and could stand on the pistil (Fig. 14(d)). The specific surface area tests show that the specific surface areas of C12-CDs-MWCNTs and C12-SCDs-MWCNTs aerogels are 275.65 and 350.05 m2/g, respectively. According to the six adsorption-desorption isotherms and four types of mesoporous hysteresis loops classified by IUPAC, it can be seen that the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the two aerogel samples belong to the “IV” type isotherms with H1 type hysteresis loops (Fig. S8). In the range of p/p 0 = 0.05–0.9, the adsorption amount increases slowly, and N2 molecules are adsorbed on the inner surface of the mesoporous in the monolayer to multilayer. When p/p 0 = 0.9–1, the adsorption amount increases abruptly, the desorption isotherm does not coincide with the adsorption isotherm, and the desorption isotherm is above the adsorption isotherm, which is mainly due to capillary condensation. At this time, the large slope of the curve indicates that the holes are evenly distributed, and the porosity is high. A hysteresis loop suggests that the material accumulates to form mesopores [54]. With the introduction of the sulfonate groups, the type of adsorption-desorption isotherm does not change. However, the adsorption amount becomes large, indicating that the pore properties of the aerogel samples do not change. However, the pore diameter and volume change, and the specific surface area become large [55]. These two amphiphilic CDs have excellent performance as dispersants. It is conceivable that other nanomaterials such as graphene, MoS2, and g-C3N4 can be further dispersed using their π-π conjugated structures and hydrophobic interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: Amphiphilic CDs (C12-CDs and C12-SCDs) were synthesized under mild reaction conditions. These CDs exhibit higher surface activity than conventional surfactants (APG12 and SDBS), while hydrophilic CDs have negligible surface activity. DLS analysis, solubilization experiments, and TEM images confirmed that C12-CDs aggregate into an irregular spherical structure like a micelle with an average diameter of about 100 nm, whereas C12-SCDs do not form aggregates in the studied conditions.

The surface tension curves show the effects of pH, temperature, and inorganic salts on the surface activity of CDs. At pH = 3, the surface tension reaches the minimum value. As pH increases, the increasing water solubility of CDs enhances the surface tension. With the increase in temperature, the hydrogen bonding between CDs and water becomes weak, decreasing the surface tension. C12-CDs can still maintain high activity at c NaCl = 250 g/L, but their resistance to CaCl2 is poor. The addition of the sulfonate groups enhances both the tolerance of CDs to NaCl and CaCl2. At c NaCl = 360 g/L and c CaCl2 = 750 g/L, C12-SCDs still have high surface activity.

The removal efficiency of oil film and contact angle experiments show that compared to the traditional surfactant SDBS, C12-CDs and C12-SCDs have an excellent wettability alternation due to their abundant amine, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and sulfonate groups. Among them, the wettability of C12-SCDs is better than that of C12-CDs due to their wealthy sulfonate groups, which are hydrophilic.

The Pickering emulsions stabilized by C12-SCDs have rapid pH-response performance. Specifically, the emulsion undergoes multiple reversible demulsification-emulsification cycles by adjusting the pH value, resulting from the hydrophile-lipophile balance of C12-SCDs repeatedly adjusted by changing pH values. Based on this principle, the emulsion also realizes CO2-response.

Compared with the traditional surfactants SDBS and APG12, CDs have an excellent dispersion performance to MWCNTs. Different from the dispersion mechanism of cationic surfactant CPC (strong electrostatic attraction), CDs are adsorbed on the surface of MWCNTs through their hydrophobic effect and π-π stacking effect to achieve effective dispersion. Among them, because the sulfonate groups have great steric resistance and electrostatic repulsion, the dispersion performance of C12-SCDs is better than C12-CDs.
